nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving or still blackwater streams, backwater pools, and seasonally flooded lowland llanos with sandy or muddy substrates, leaf litter, and tannin-stained water

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate for natural digging and sifting
coconut-shell caves, clay pots, or commercial cichlid caves (one per female minimum)
driftwood and twisted roots for cover and territory division
Indian almond (catappa) leaves and leaf litter for tannins
dense planting with Java Fern, Anubias, and Cryptocoryne
floating plants to diffuse light and reduce stress

diet.notes: Primarily carnivorous; wild diet consists of small invertebrates, microcrustaceans, insect larvae, and detritus. In captivity, a staple of frozen and live foods is essential — bloodworms, brine shrimp, daphnia, cyclops, and mysis shrimp. High-quality micro-pellets or cichlid flakes with krill or insect meal can supplement but cannot replace live/frozen foods, which are important for peak coloration and conditioning for breeding. Feed small amounts 1–2 times daily.

goodWith: small Corydoras species (C. habrosus, C. pygmaeus)
avoidWith: other male A. hongsloi unless tank is 115+ L with full sight-line breaks
fast, boisterous species (Buenos Aires tetras, giant danios)
large plecos that may disturb caves

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Apistogramma hongsloi; verify current URL at fishbase.se/summary/Apistogramma-hongsloi.html before use.

summary: A strikingly red Colombian Orinoco dwarf cichlid belonging to the macmasteri group. Males display vivid red-orange body color with blue facial markings; captive color forms include 'Super Red' and 'Original/Wild'. Polygynous cave-spawners with intense female brood care. Best kept in soft, acidic blackwater with one male and two or more females.
careNotes: Apistogramma hongsloi (Rotstrich) is a member of the macmasteri species group within Apistogramma, endemic to the Orinoco basin in Colombia and Venezuela — primarily the Río Vichada and Río Meta drainages within the seasonally flooded Llanos lowlands. Males reach up to 7.5 cm and develop intense red-orange coloration across the ventral half of the body with striking blue facial streaking; females are smaller (to ~4.5 cm) and turn bright yellow when guarding eggs. Multiple captive color forms are commonly available in the trade: the 'Original' or 'Wild' form reflects the natural coloration of wild-caught specimens; the 'Super Red' is a domestically line-bred strain with intensified red-orange pigmentation produced by selective crossing of naturally vivid wild specimens and regional cf. hongsloi forms over many generations; 'Red Mask' (occasionally applied to A. hongsloi in trade) more precisely describes a parallel strain of the closely related A. macmasteri — purchasers should verify the species label when sourcing. Water quality is the single most important factor for color expression: soft, acidic water (pH 5.0–7.0, gH 1–10) and warmer temperatures (26–29°C) bring out the most intense red pigmentation. Tannin-stained water via Indian almond leaves and driftwood closely replicates the natural blackwater environment and reduces stress. Maintain 0 ppm ammonia/nitrite and nitrate below 20 ppm; small, frequent water changes are preferable to large ones to avoid parameter shock. The tank should provide at least one cave per female (coconut shells, clay pots, or commercial cichlid caves), plus driftwood, dense planting, and broken sight lines. A minimum 75 L tank for a single pair; 115 L or more for a harem of one male and 2–3 females.
breedingNotes: A. hongsloi is a polygynous (haremic) cave-spawner with exclusive female brood care. Under good conditions, breeding proceeds without difficulty — soft, acidic water (pH 5.5–6.5) and conditioning with live or frozen foods reliably trigger spawning. The female selects a cave and deposits 60–90 adhesive eggs on the cave ceiling; the male patrols the surrounding territory. Eggs hatch in approximately 2–3 days at breeding temperature; wrigglers remain in the cave for a further 4–5 days before becoming free-swimming. The female becomes highly protective during this period and may aggressively defend fry against the male and other tankmates. Free-swimming fry accept freshly hatched baby brine shrimp and microworms from the outset. A separate breeding tank of 40–75 L with a single cave, soft sand, and botanicals simplifies raising fry to juvenile stage. Fry begin showing color differentiation at approximately 6–8 weeks.

primary.name: Aquarium Store Depot — Hongsloi Apisto Care Guide
primary.notes: Primary trade care source. Provided temperature (24–28°C), pH (5.0–7.0), gH (1–8 dGH), kH (1–5 dKH), tank size (75 L pair / 115 L harem), male size (6–7.5 cm), female size (4–4.5 cm), lifespan (3–5 yr), native range (Orinoco / Río Vichada / Río Meta, Colombia and Venezuela), egg count (60–90), hatching time (2–3 days), decor, diet, compatibility, and haremic breeding structure. Egg-ceiling attachment and female brood care confirmed.

additional.name: FishBase — Apistogramma hongsloi
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes, Geophaginae), maximum size (7.0 cm TL; males 3.4 cm SL), temperature (23–30°C), pH (5.5–6.0), native range (Colombia: Orinoco basin, Vichada River drainage, middle Meta River drainage; middle Orinoco), trophic level 3.3 (carnivore/omnivore). Egg ceiling-attachment in caves confirmed. Taxonomy note: FishBase places in Cichliformes; some older sources use Perciformes — Cichliformes used as current consensus.

additional.name: Steenfott Aquatics — Apistogramma hongsloi: A Comprehensive Guide
additional.notes: Corroborated pH (5.5–7.0), gH (0–8 dGH), blackwater/Orinoco habitat. Noted 'Hongsloi II' / Super Red as intensified captive line. Emphasized soft water as prerequisite for peak color. No lifespan stated.

additional.name: Apistogramma.com Forum — Super Red Hongsloi Origin
additional.notes: Expert forum thread (mod: Mike Wise) on the origin of the Super Red Hongsloi captive strain. Confirmed that Super Red is a domestic strain produced from A. hongsloi (Rotstrich) crossed with multiple A. cf. hongsloi regional forms from Colombia and Venezuela by successive breeders. Clarified that 'Red Mask' is more precisely a A. macmasteri strain, not an A. hongsloi color form. Confirmed macmasteri group membership.

additional.name: DwarfCichlid.com — Apistogramma hongsloi
additional.notes: Provided habitat context (tributaries of Río Orinoco, Colombia and Venezuela), confirmed acceptance of live/frozen foods, cave-spawner behavior, biparental care reference, and multiple named wild cf. hongsloi variants (A110–A115). Noted species is 'fairly robust' and easy to breed with standard techniques.

additional.name: aquifarm.com — Apistogramma hongsloi Care
additional.notes: Additional corroboration on temperature (24–29°C), pH (5.5–7.0), gH (1–10 dGH), soft sandy substrate, cave-spawning, female brood care, Super Red color form, and compatibility lists.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave-spawner; 60–90 eggs deposited on cave ceiling. Female provides exclusive brood care and becomes highly protective of wrigglers and free-swimming fry. Polygynous — keep one male with 2–3 females for natural haremic behavior. Conditioning with live/frozen foods and soft acidic water (pH 5.5–6.5) reliably triggers spawning. Fry accept baby brine shrimp from free-swimming stage.
